greeting.greeting: Hey, good to see you. So I'm Kavya — I'm on the analytics side here at Zomato. Before we dig in, I want to understand what you're actually hoping to get out of our time together today. Is there a specific part of the role or the work itself you want to make sure we cover?

audio_segments.text: So I'm Kavya — I'm on the analytics side here at Zomato.
Before we dig in, I want to understand what you're actually hoping to get out of our time together today.
Is there a specific part of the role or the work itself you want to make sure we cover?

greeting.interviewer_identity: Earnest mentor, deliberate pauses, plain-spoken, leads with context then asks, curious about candidate's own learning curve, sits in the craft itself

turns.reply: That energy dashboard sounds like real work — twelve buildings is a decent scope. Let me ask you this: when you were building that, what was the messiest part of getting the data ready, and how did you end up solving it?

Good instinct there — flagging instead of dropping means you kept the signal and gave someone downstream visibility into what was broken. That's exactly the kind of thinking we need.

Let's switch gears. In a real scenario at Zomato, we get delivery data streaming in from thousands of restaurants and riders across the country. Some orders have missing delivery times, some have rider GPS pings that don't line up with the actual route, some timestamps are out of order because of network lag. You're building a pipeline to feed this into a model that predicts delivery time.

Walk me through: how would you decide what to do with the messy data — and what would you actually measure to know if your cleaning approach was working?

That's a sharp instinct — you're right that the denominator is where the trap door opens.

But let me push a bit deeper on the delivery time piece. Say you've decided the pipeline feeds a model that needs to predict arrival time for the customer app. Now you've got three choices: drop orders with missing delivery times, estimate them using nearby orders, or mark them as a separate category and handle them differently in the model.

What would actually break if you picked the wrong one — and how would you know which one to pick?

That's the right reflex — catching your own mistakes before they reach someone else. You built a system to prevent it happening again.

Here's the thing though: in that delivery time scenario, you're not just trying to avoid double-counts. You're deciding what data actually goes into a model that hundreds of thousands of people will see an ETA from.

So let me ask differently: if you dropped all the messy delivery records, what would that do to your model's predictions for certain kinds of orders — say, orders from new restaurants, or late-night deliveries, or during monsoon when GPS is spotty?

You're asking a fair question back — I like that. But let me stay with this one for a moment, because it's the real craft.

Think about it this way: if you silently drop messy records, your model trains on a cleaned-up version of reality. So when it goes live and sees *actual* messy data — which it will — it's predicting on something it's never seen before.

What does that mean for certain kinds of orders? And how would you catch it?

That's exactly right — you made the decision on evidence, not ego. That matters.

Let's shift gears now. Tell me about a time when you built something — a dashboard, a pipeline, a script, whatever it was — and it broke in production or didn't work the way you expected. Walk me through what happened, what you did about it, and what you learned from it.

You're thinking in cohorts — that's solid. You're also spotting the pattern that early churn is usually friction, not product fit.

But I want to go back to something real. You mentioned the energy dashboard you built last semester. Walk me through a specific moment where something didn't work the way you expected — maybe the data didn't load right, or a calculation was off, or someone using it found a bug. What actually happened, and how did you handle it?

That's the right move — you're not guessing, you're asking the query plan to tell you what's actually slow before you change anything.

Here's a real situation for you: you're on call one night and a dashboard your team owns starts running thirty minutes slower than usual. No code changed, no schema changed — the data is just the same. You've got maybe two minutes before stakeholders start pinging. What's your first move, and why that one?

That's useful self-awareness — you know what moves you. The fact that you *rebuilt* the whole dashboard instead of just tweaking it says you took the hit seriously.

Let's switch gears to something scenario-based. Picture this: you're three weeks into the job, and you've built a SQL query that pulls customer retention numbers for the leadership team's weekly sync. The numbers show a two percent drop week-on-week. Leadership is about to present them to investors. Then one of your teammates casually asks, "Did you check if that cohort definition matches what product is using?" You haven't. You pull the code — and it doesn't.

You've got maybe twenty minutes before the meeting. Walk me through what you do, step by step.

That's exactly the kind of detail that tells me you've actually thought about how we work — not just Googled "Zomato data" the night before. Variance optimization is a real constraint we live in.

You've got questions for me now, yeah? What do you want to know about the role, the team, or how we actually work here?

I appreciate that — you're naming something real and you've already built a system to manage it. Time-boxing the first pass is smart; it forces you to ship something that's *useful* before you ship something that's *perfect*. That's exactly the tension we live in here.

So: what do you want to ask me?

That's the exact right metric — you're measuring adoption, not aesthetics. If people are still emailing you every Monday asking for the same number, the dashboard didn't actually change their workflow. That's a clean way to think about it.

Alright, so I've asked you a lot. Let me flip it: what would you change about how this interview went for you? What was useful, what wasn't, what would you do differently if you were running it?

You caught the operational gap — flagging without a process to act on it really is just noise, and tying it to a revenue threshold means you're surfacing something the business actually cares about. That's the kind of thinking that separates a pipeline from a *useful* pipeline.

But I asked you something just now — how did this session feel for you? What landed, what didn't, what would you take away from it?

messages.content: Hey, good to see you. So I'm Kavya — I'm on the analytics side here at Zomato. Before we dig in, I want to understand what you're actually hoping to get out of our time together today. Is there a specific part of the role or the work itself you want to make sure we cover?

Sure. I'm a final-year computer science student and I've spent the last year working on data pipelines, mostly in Python and SQL. Last semester I built a dashboard that tracked campus energy use across twelve buildings.

That energy dashboard sounds like real work — twelve buildings is a decent scope. Let me ask you this: when you were building that, what was the messiest part of getting the data ready, and how did you end up solving it?

The hardest part was the data quality. Meter readings arrived with gaps and duplicate timestamps, so I wrote a reconciliation step that flagged anomalies instead of silently dropping them. That cut bad rows by about eighty percent.

Good instinct there — flagging instead of dropping means you kept the signal and gave someone downstream visibility into what was broken. That's exactly the kind of thinking we need.

Let's switch gears. In a real scenario at Zomato, we get delivery data streaming in from thousands of restaurants and riders across the country. Some orders have missing delivery times, some have rider GPS pings that don't line up with the actual route, some timestamps are out of order because of network lag. You're building a pipeline to feed this into a model that predicts delivery time.

Walk me through: how would you decide what to do with the messy data — and what would you actually measure to know if your cleaning approach was working?

I'd start by asking what decision the number is meant to support. If nobody can answer that, the metric is decoration. Then I'd check the denominator, because that's where most misleading percentages hide.

That's a sharp instinct — you're right that the denominator is where the trap door opens.

But let me push a bit deeper on the delivery time piece. Say you've decided the pipeline feeds a model that needs to predict arrival time for the customer app. Now you've got three choices: drop orders with missing delivery times, estimate them using nearby orders, or mark them as a separate category and handle them differently in the model.

What would actually break if you picked the wrong one — and how would you know which one to pick?

I once shipped a query that double-counted refunds. Finance caught it, not me. I wrote a regression test for it the same day and started reviewing my own joins against a known-total check before shipping.

That's the right reflex — catching your own mistakes before they reach someone else. You built a system to prevent it happening again.

Here's the thing though: in that delivery time scenario, you're not just trying to avoid double-counts. You're deciding what data actually goes into a model that hundreds of thousands of people will see an ETA from.

So let me ask differently: if you dropped all the messy delivery records, what would that do to your model's predictions for certain kinds of orders — say, orders from new restaurants, or late-night deliveries, or during monsoon when GPS is spotty?
What does the first ninety days look like for someone in this seat, and how would you know at the end of it whether the hire was a good one?

You're asking a fair question back — I like that. But let me stay with this one for a moment, because it's the real craft.

Think about it this way: if you silently drop messy records, your model trains on a cleaned-up version of reality. So when it goes live and sees *actual* messy data — which it will — it's predicting on something it's never seen before.

What does that mean for certain kinds of orders? And how would you catch it?

In my last internship I disagreed with my manager about caching a report. I pulled the query logs, showed that the underlying table only changed twice a day, and we settled it on the numbers rather than on seniority.

That's exactly right — you made the decision on evidence, not ego. That matters.

Let's shift gears now. Tell me about a time when you built something — a dashboard, a pipeline, a script, whatever it was — and it broke in production or didn't work the way you expected. Walk me through what happened, what you did about it, and what you learned from it.

I'd segment by cohort first. A blended retention curve hides the fact that the January cohort might be churning while March looks healthy. Then I'd look at the second-week drop, because that's usually onboarding rather than product.

You're thinking in cohorts — that's solid. You're also spotting the pattern that early churn is usually friction, not product fit.

But I want to go back to something real. You mentioned the energy dashboard you built last semester. Walk me through a specific moment where something didn't work the way you expected — maybe the data didn't load right, or a calculation was off, or someone using it found a bug. What actually happened, and how did you handle it?
Honestly, I don't know that one. My instinct would be to look at how the index is being used before adding another, but I'd want to check the query plan rather than guess in the room.

That's the right move — you're not guessing, you're asking the query plan to tell you what's actually slow before you change anything.

Here's a real situation for you: you're on call one night and a dashboard your team owns starts running thirty minutes slower than usual. No code changed, no schema changed — the data is just the same. You've got maybe two minutes before stakeholders start pinging. What's your first move, and why that one?

I'd rather be told directly. In my project reviews the feedback that actually changed how I work was the blunt kind — being told my charts were pretty and unreadable made me rebuild the whole dashboard.

That's useful self-awareness — you know what moves you. The fact that you *rebuilt* the whole dashboard instead of just tweaking it says you took the hit seriously.

Let's switch gears to something scenario-based. Picture this: you're three weeks into the job, and you've built a SQL query that pulls customer retention numbers for the leadership team's weekly sync. The numbers show a two percent drop week-on-week. Leadership is about to present them to investors. Then one of your teammates casually asks, "Did you check if that cohort definition matches what product is using?" You haven't. You pull the code — and it doesn't.

You've got maybe twenty minutes before the meeting. Walk me through what you do, step by step.

I read the engineering blog and the piece on your delivery-time model. The part that stuck with me was that you optimise for variance, not just the mean, because a late order hurts more than an early one helps.

That's exactly the kind of detail that tells me you've actually thought about how we work — not just Googled "Zomato data" the night before. Variance optimization is a real constraint we live in.

You've got questions for me now, yeah? What do you want to know about the role, the team, or how we actually work here?
My weakness is that I go deep before I go wide — I'll perfect one query while the wider question is still open. I've started time-boxing the first pass to get to an answer, then refining it.

I appreciate that — you're naming something real and you've already built a system to manage it. Time-boxing the first pass is smart; it forces you to ship something that's *useful* before you ship something that's *perfect*. That's exactly the tension we live in here.

So: what do you want to ask me?
I'd measure success by whether the team stopped asking for the report manually. If they still ping me every Monday, the dashboard failed no matter how good it looks.

That's the exact right metric — you're measuring adoption, not aesthetics. If people are still emailing you every Monday asking for the same number, the dashboard didn't actually change their workflow. That's a clean way to think about it.

Alright, so I've asked you a lot. Let me flip it: what would you change about how this interview went for you? What was useful, what wasn't, what would you do differently if you were running it?

That's a fair challenge. I think the risk with my approach is that flagging anomalies creates a queue nobody works. So I'd pair it with a threshold that auto-escalates anything above two percent of revenue.

You caught the operational gap — flagging without a process to act on it really is just noise, and tying it to a revenue threshold means you're surfacing something the business actually cares about. That's the kind of thinking that separates a pipeline from a *useful* pipeline.

But I asked you something just now — how did this session feel for you? What landed, what didn't, what would you take away from it?

reask_mute.reply: You're on mute — go ahead and unmute, or if you'd rather type out your answer, that works just as well and we'll keep moving.
